coredata.dc:title: Anthraquinone-rich Rheum ribes L. as a source of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for ZnO-based S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts in tetracycline degradation

coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis has emerged as a promising, sustainable solution to address environmental pollution caused by industrial growth and increasing societal demands. An ideal photocatalyst requires both strong redox capabilities and environmentally benign materials. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized from anthraquinone-rich Rheum ribes L., using an eco-friendly approach that leverages natural sources. These N-CQDs were then combined with ZnO to form a heterojunction photocatalyst for the degradation of tetracycline (TC) as a model pollutant under UV light irradiation. The N-CQDs/ZnO composite exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to its individual components, attributed to the robust interactions between the polar functional groups of N-CQDs and ZnO, as verified through advanced characterization techniques. The photocatalytic performance of the composite was systematically assessed by varying operational parameters (pH, temperature, catalyst/pollutant amount), and optimization of the N-CQDs content within the heterojunction produced a synergistic effect, increasing light absorption and promoting efficient charge separation through an S-scheme heterojunction mechanism. With the highly efficient catalyst in hand, the scope of pollutants was expanded to include dyes, heavy metals, and various antibiotics, demonstrating the catalyst's broad applicability. Radical trapping experiments further suggested a photooxidation mechanism while GC-MS results showed the intermediates transition products during the degradation of TC. These findings indicate that the N-CQDs/ZnO heterojunction is a highly effective photocatalyst for antibiotic contaminant removal, offering promising potential for advanced water treatment applications.

head.ce:title: Anthraquinone-rich Rheum ribes L. as a source of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for ZnO-based S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts in tetracycline degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis has emerged as a promising, sustainable solution to address environmental pollution caused by industrial growth and increasing societal demands. An ideal photocatalyst requires both strong redox capabilities and environmentally benign materials. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized from anthraquinone-rich Rheum ribes L., using an eco-friendly approach that leverages natural sources. These N-CQDs were then combined with ZnO to form a heterojunction photocatalyst for the degradation of tetracycline (TC) as a model pollutant under UV light irradiation. The N-CQDs/ZnO composite exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to its individual components, attributed to the robust interactions between the polar functional groups of N-CQDs and ZnO, as verified through advanced characterization techniques. The photocatalytic performance of the composite was systematically assessed by varying operational parameters (pH, temperature, catalyst/pollutant amount), and optimization of the N-CQDs content within the heterojunction produced a synergistic effect, increasing light absorption and promoting efficient charge separation through an S-scheme heterojunction mechanism. With the highly efficient catalyst in hand, the scope of pollutants was expanded to include dyes, heavy metals, and various antibiotics, demonstrating the catalyst's broad applicability. Radical trapping experiments further suggested a photooxidation mechanism while GC-MS results showed the intermediates transition products during the degradation of TC. These findings indicate that the N-CQDs/ZnO heterojunction is a highly effective photocatalyst for antibiotic contaminant removal, offering promising potential for advanced water treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by hazardous organic pollutants discharged from paper, leather, textile, medicinal, and food industries poses a significant challenge to sustainable societal development [1]. Addressing water pollution has become crucial, as researchers worldwide have invested enormous efforts to tackling these issues, including antibiotic contamination. Tetracycline (TC) is one of the most often used antibiotics, serving as a medication, feed additive, and antibacterial agent. Furthermore, due to its antimicrobial and refractory qualities, TC cannot be completely degraded by standard wastewater treatment techniques [2]. The leftover TC is toxic to many aquatic organisms, causing medication resistance and complicating antibiotic therapy [3,4]. Tetracycline can accumulate along the food chain, causing toxicity to the microbial community, stimulating the development and spread of antibiotic resistance, posing hazards to drinking and irrigation water, and changing microbial flora in the human intestine [5]. As a result, it is critical to develop effective and practical treatment strategies for TC degradation in water. Photocatalytic oxidation reaction, a green technique, appears to be a cost-effective, high-performance, and environmentally sustainable alternative in the treatment of tetracycline in wastewater under UV light/visible light irradiation [6]. However, the efficiency of photocatalytic processes is greatly determined by the catalyst utilized. Given the efforts made to improve the photocatalytic effectiveness of catalysts and the number of studies published each year, it is apparent that there is still a significant need for innovative catalyst designs in this field.

Zinc oxide (ZnO) has gained a lot of attention among accessible photocatalysts due to its high photosensitivity, low cost, ease of synthesis, environmental friendliness, and optoelectronic properties that can be easily adjustable by various types of doping or heterojunction creation [7,8]. Recent research show that ZnO has enhanced photocatalytic activity under UV-light for degrading organic pollutants in aqueous solution [1,8,9]. ZnO absorbs a large portion of the UV spectrum and has a comparatively high excitation binding energy of 60 meV. When exposed to UV radiation, it oxidizes better than TiO2 due to its wider range of UV spectrum absorption. Hence, using ZnO as a photocatalyst enables the complete mineralization of pollutants in wastewater [10]. Studying the photocatalytic degradation of doxycycline (DOX) under UV light by preparing the ZnO/TiO2 photocatalyst, Ani et al.[10] found that 50 ppm and 100 ppm DOX were degraded within 30 and 100 min, respectively. Kohantorabi et al. [11]. studied the photocatalytic oxidation of acetaminophen (ACT) activated by peroxymonosulfate (PMS) under UVA irradiation by preparing Ag/ZnO@NiFe2O4 catalyst. The researchers found that 62.2 % of ACT could be removed in the NiFe2O4/UVA/PMS system, and 100 % removal was achieved within 15 min when ZnO was added to the system. However, difficulties such as the quick recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, ZnO’s narrow absorption band, and photocorrosion continue to limit its usage as a photocatalyst [1,12]. Increased specific surface area and electron hole-directed transport channels may improve the rate and efficacy of photocatalytic processes. Scientists have proposed a variety of techniques to solve these challenges, including elemental doping, surface modification and functionalization, cocatalyst loading, and heterojunction assembly [13,14]. In particular, heterojunction design, that allows for effective interfacial charge transfer, is commonly regarded as a valuable method in catalyst manufacturing since it prevents recombination while extending the lifetime of the charge carriers [14]. Quantum dot sensitization involves forming heterojunctions by coupling photocatalysts with quantum dots that possess complementary physicochemical properties, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [15]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently emerged as a strategy for increasing semiconductor photocatalytic performance. CQDs are nanomaterials made up of carbonaceous skeletons that frequently feature sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon atoms having polar functional groups, with a size of smaller than 10 nm [16]. They offer outstanding properties such as strong conductivity, wideband light absorption, low toxicity, high photoluminescence emission, chemical stability, compatibility with a variety of components, and water dispersibility. Furthermore, the abundant availability of biomass from various sources makes their synthesis straightforward and cost-effective [17]. The successful incorporation of CQDs into ZnO can increase light absorption, utilization, and charge separation [18]. However, CQDs must have a high quantum yield and photoluminescence emission capacity; otherwise, their contribution to the catalyst's photocatalytic activity may be limited. Quantum dots can be doped with earth-abundant elements like nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus to solve this issue [17]. Nowadays, N doping in CQDs has a significant impact on tuning the intrinsic properties of CQDs and improving their surface chemical reactivities, fluorescent properties as well as optoelectronic capabilities. Owing to nonbonding electrons on N, N-doped CQD may easily generate charge delocalization, lower the work function of nanodots, and significantly boost electron transferability. To date, CQDs have been synthesized using various methods, including chemical ablation [19], electrochemical carbonization [20], laser ablation [21], microwave irradiation [22], and hydrothermal treatment [23]. It is critical to study the impact of precursors on the properties of CQDs, particularly their photocatalytic activities after coupling with ZnO [24]. Several raw materials containing a high number of carbon precursors, such as citric acid [23], glucose [24], sucrose [25], lime (Citrus aurantifolia) [26], gingko biloba [27] have previously been used in the fabrication of CQDs owing to their complex organic structures.

The Polygonaceae family's Rheum ribes L., by locals in Bingöl, Erzurum, Hakkari, and Erzincan, is the only native Rheum species that thrives in East and Southeast Anatolia. Rheum species are medically important because they contain anthraquinone-derived chemicals, and Rheum ribes is also utilized to make numerous herbal remedies in the Middle East [28]. Anthraquinones have outstanding redox-active and photosensitive capabilities, making them widely used as photocatalysts in a variety of sectors, including medicine, energy, optics, and synthetic chemistry. One key advantage of anthraquinones is the ability to manage their structural changes, which influence their redox and photochemical activities [29].

In this study, N-CQDs were synthesized from anthraquinone-rich Rheum ribes L. and combined with ZnO to create a heterojunction for the photocatalytic degradation of TC under UV light irradiation. This strong interaction was validated using advanced characterization techniques. The N-CQDs/ZnO composite demonstrated significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to the individual components, attributed to the robust interactions between the polar functional groups of N-CQDs and ZnO. The photocatalytic performance of the N-CQDs/ZnO composite was systematically evaluated by varying operational parameters such as catalyst concentration, TC concentration, and initial solution pH. Additionally, optimization of the N-CQDs content in the heterojunction was performed to maximize the synergistic effect between N-CQDs and ZnO. The photodegradation efficiency improved to 78.33 %, with a reaction rate of 1.12 X 10−2 min−1, making it 1.56 times faster than ZnO. Furthermore, the catalyst maintained its chemical stability and long-term durability across five reuse cycles, with no reduction in degradation efficiency, indicating the robustness of the active sites on the N-CQDs/ZnO surface. The enhanced photocatalytic activity was linked to the strong coupling at the nanomaterial interfaces, which facilitated increased light absorption and promoted effective charge separation through the S-scheme heterojunction mechanism. Radical trapping experiments further suggested a potential photooxidation mechanism in the degradation process. The degradation of TC likely proceeds through a photooxidation mechanism, primarily influenced by

species. This synthesis strategy enabled efficient charge separation and transfer, yielding superior photocatalytic performance compared to bare ZnO.

The synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was carried out using a hydrothermal method. For this, 6.25 mL of HNO3 (68 wt%), 4.75 mL of distilled water, 0.3 g urea and 9 mL of ethylene glycol were mixed with a magnetic stirrer for 15 min. Then, 0.5 g of Rheum Ribes (rhubarb) was added. After an additional 15 min of magnetic stirring, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept in a temperature-controlled oven at 150 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was then cooled to room temperature, and the obtained product was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 minutes. To remove unreacted organic fractions and unwanted large particles from the obtained solution, it was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter. Finally, the purified N-CQDs were collected in a clean glass bottle, dried at approximately 75 °C for 5 hours, and stored at 5 °C for use in experiments [30].

2.64 g of zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O) was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water and the solution was stirred for 10 minutes. 4.8 g of NaOH was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and added dropwise to the solution, which was then stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 10 minutes. The resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 6 hours. The final product was obtained by filtration and drying at 60 °C [30].

The N-CQDs/ZnO catalyst was synthesized using a hydrothermal method. In the first step, 2.64 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O was dissolved in 60 mL distilled water. Additionally, 0.315 g of hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) and 2 M NaOH solutions were prepared in 10 mL of distilled water. As described above, a 5 mL N-CQDs solution was prepared, to which 5 mL of water was added to obtain a homogeneous mixture. This mixture was then added to the zinc-containing solution and stirred for 10 min. The resulting mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with an internal volume of 100 mL. It was subjected to a controlled temperature reaction at 150 °C for 6 hours in a muffle furnace. After the reaction, the suspension was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 minutes. The obtained solution was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 7–12 hours. Finally, the obtained N-CQDs/ZnO catalyst was stored in sealed containers for use in experiments [30]. Figure S1 schematically depicts the catalyst synthesis.

After the construction of heterojunction, all the samples were characterized before photocatalytic performance. To investigate the crystallinity of the samples, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of as-synthesized samples of N-CQDs, ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite was performed as shown Figure S2. The diffractogram of the N-CQDs shows the broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20.91˚; this corresponds to a highly polycrystalline (amorphous) carbon structure, implying that the hydrothermal process leads to the formation of rich active centers on the surface of the N-CQDs [16,31,32]. The depicted peaks for ZnO nanocatalyst at 2θ = 31.81˚, 34.44˚, 36.27˚, 47.57˚, 56.62˚, 62.88˚, 66.39˚, 67.97˚, 69.10˚, 72.57˚, and 77.03˚ corresponded to the hexagonal wurtzite structure are found in the crystalline planes of (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004), and (202) respectively (JCPD36–1451) [33,34]. No change in the peak positions in the XRD diffractogram of N-CQDs/ZnO reveals that the addition of N-CQDs did not have a significant effect on the crystallinity and phase purity of the nanocomposite [32]. The reduction in peak intensities in the XRD patterns of the N-CQDs/ZnO composite, compared to pure ZnO, without apparent shifts in peak positions, suggests that the CQD particles do not integrate into the ZnO matrix but instead preferentially adsorb at the grain boundaries of ZnO crystals along the high-energy (100) and (101) planes [32,35]. No peaks of N-CQDs were observed in the XRD diffractogram of N-CQDs/ZnO, probably due to its low amount or its amorphous nature [33]. Particle size was computed from the peak corresponding to d100 plane using Debye–Scherrer equation [35], and it was defined to be 26.91 nm and 26.88 nm for ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposites, respectively. From the Debye-scherrer equation, the d spacing of the (100) peak at 31.7˚ of ZnO is 0.29 nm, which is slightly higher than the hexagonal wurtzite structure (0.28 nm), and the d spacing of the 101 peak at 36.19˚ is 0.26 nm, which is in agreement with the wurtzite crystal structure [36].

To confirm the incorporation of N-CQDs into the ZnO structure, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analyses of the synthesized N-CQDs, ZnO and CQDs/ZnO nanocatalysts were performed and the results are displayed in Figure S3. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1093 cm−1, 3654 cm−1, and within the range of 3002–3203 cm−1 represents the presence of stretching vibration of the O–H group present at the surface of the N-CQD [36]. Additionally, the peaks observed at 2865 cm−1, 2958 cm−1, and 1326 cm−1 correspond to C–H stretching and C–N vibration modes [37,38], while the peaks at 1080 cm−1 and 1664 cm−1 are indicative of C–O stretching mode and CO vibration, respectively [8,27,38]. Moreover, the peaks observed at 619 cm−1, 869 cm−1, and 653 cm−1 can be attributed to aromatic compounds with sp2 hybridizations, strong =C–H bending, and aromatic sp2 C–H bending vibrations, respectively [17,37]. The FT-IR spectrum (Figure S3) of the pure ZnO photocatalyst exhibited two peaks at 445 and 1394 cm–1 assigned to the Zn–O stretching vibration and the bending vibration of the bridging hydroxyl on the surface of ZnO, respectively [18]. In the FTIR spectrum of the N-CQDs/ZnO composite, the distinctly shifting of the Zn-O vibrational band at 445 cm−1 to 459 cm−1 after the combination may have been caused by the reaction between the carboxyl group in the N-CQDs and the hydroxyl group on the surface of ZnO. This gives us another proof for the successful introduction of N-CQDs into the ZnO structure [39].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to verify the chemical structure and surface compositions of N-CQDs, ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO nanocatalysts as shown in Fig. 1 a-c and Figure S4a and b. The observed peaks at 285.08, 401.08 and 532.07 eV in the survey spectrum of the N-CQDs assigned to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Figure S4a). The deconvolution of high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Figure S4b), revealed the presence of CN-C at 397.84 eV, N-C3 at 399.01 eV, and -NH2 at 400.02 eV [40,41]. In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1 a), the peaks at 284.50, 285.89, and 288.23 eV correspond to C-C/CC, C-N/C-O, CN/CO bonds, respectively while the peaks at 531.01 and 531.95 eV in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 b), assigned to C–OH/C–O–C and CO bonds. These results proposed that the as-prepared N-CQDs have abundant hydrophilic groups at the surface [42,43]. On the other hand, the O 1 s peaks of ZnO were dissociated into two peaks with binding energies of 529.88 eV and 531.48 eV attributing to the O2− ions in the wurtzite-structured ZnO and the oxygen-deficient region (O defect) in the ZnO lattice, respectively [44]. According to the survey XPS spectra shown in Figure S4a, N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite involves C, O, Zn and N elements confirming the successful doping of as-prepared N-CQDs on the ZnO surface. The peaks appeared at 529.90 and 531.41, 1021.31 and 1044.38, 284.46, 286.12, and 288.42, and 401.08 eV ascribed to O 1 s, Zn 2p, C 1 s, and N 1 s, respectively, approved the formation of heterojunction compound. The deconvoluted C 1 s core level spectrum of N-CQDs/ZnO heterojunction in Fig. 1 a demonstrated three peaks centered at binding energies of 284.50, 286.12 and 288.42 eV. The drastic increase in the binding energy values of C-N/C-O and CN/CO after the integration of N-CQDs into the ZnO structure clearly reveals the strong interaction between the two structures. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite (Fig. 1 b) has two peaks at the binding energy values of 529.9 and 531.41 eV while the peaks of Zn 2p doublets for the N-CQDs/ZnO sample positioned at the 1021.30 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and 1044.28 eV (Zn 2p1/2) [32] (Fig. 1 c). When the O 1 s binding energies of the composite sample were compared with the O 1 s binding energies in both N-CQDs and ZnO, there was a shift towards lower binding energy. Meanwhile, Zn 2p binding energies shifted to lower energy regions in the composite sample compared to the ZnO sample. Considering the changes in peak intensities and binding energies, N-CQDs were successfully immobilized on the ZnO surface and there is an electron flow from N-CQDs to ZnO.

The morphological and compositional properties of the as-prepared samples were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM), along with elemental mapping. The TEM image of N-CQDs in Fig. 2 a reveals that the N-CQD particles are nearly spherical in shape, with average size in the range of 4–9 nm, and their monodisperse distribution. In Fig. 2 b, the selected strategy led to the formation of ZnO nanorods with varying lengths. Fig. 2 c and d demonstrate the successful incorporation of N-CQDs onto the ZnO surface, providing clear evidence that the N-CQD nanoparticles were effectively embedded onto the ZnO substrate via hydrothermal synthesis while preserving their shape and size. The HAADF-STEM image, along with the associated elemental mapping, as shown in Fig. 2 e and f confirmed the presence of Zn, O, C, and N atoms, verifying the formation of the desired composition and their homogeneous distribution.

The surface morphologies of the as-prepared samples were also examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis in different magnitudes as shown in Figure S5. The SEM image of powder N-CQDs in Figure S5a shows continuous and folded layer architectures that resemble graphite. Figure S5b and c are SEM images at different magnificence of synthesized ZnO showing rod-like structure of various lengths. It is clear from Figure S5d that after modification with N-CQDs, the ZnO crystals did not change either in shape or size, as confirmed by the XRD and TEM analyses. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) analysis was used to further determine the distributions of elements (Figure S5e -h). The EDS analysis indicated that the N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite is composed of C, O, Zn, and N. The mass percentage of C, O, Zn and N are 15.87 %, 18.03 %, 65.46 %, and 0.65 %, respectively revealing the successful formation of N-CQDs/ZnO heterojunction.

The surface areas and pore size distributions of the resulting N-CQDs, ZnO, and N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposites were analyzed utilizing BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) and BJH (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda) and the related nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms as illustrated in Figure S6 and Table S1. Figure S6a and b reveals that N-CQDs, ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO samples demonstrated Type II isotherm with H3 hysteresis, according to the IUPAC classification [45]. Based on the isotherm and hysteresis types, the catalysts exhibit a mesoporous structure, with the presence of H3-type hysteresis indicating the existence of slit-shaped pores that contribute to a more compact structure [46]. The hysteresis loop was not fully closed for all three samples, a phenomenon attributed to the swelling effect, which may occur due to the adsorption and expansion of pore walls within the mesoporous structure [47]. Pore-size distributions of the samples were determined by applying the BJH method to the desorption branch of the isotherm curves. As seen in Table S1, the BET surface areas of N-CQDs, ZnO, N-CQDs/ZnO catalysts are 0.05, 52.35 and 189.38 m2/g and the surface area of ZnO increased remarkably after immobilization. This can be attributed to a decrease in the pore diameter as a result of the dispersion of N-CQDs in the ZnO structure, whereas the 4.74 nm pore radius of ZnO decreased to 4.58 nm (Table S1). Decreasing pore radius caused the surface area of the catalyst to expand as well as the pore volume increased. The pore volume increases from 0.086 cm3/g in ZnO to 0.291 cm3/g in N-CQDs/ZnO, and this enhancement in surface area and pore volume has a positive impact on the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst.

Before photocatalytic oxidation performance of the prepared N-CQDs, ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO nanocatalysts, their optical properties were investigated. UV–vis–NIR diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was used to examine the optical properties of N-CQDs, ZnO nanorods and N-CQDs/ZnO nanocatalyst ( Fig. 3 a and b) [48]. Absorption spectrum of bare N-CQDs solution exhibit two broad peak at 255 nm and 316 nm belonging to the π- π* transition of the aromatic carbon (sp2), and n-π* transition of the CN, CO, and C-N bonds, respectively, confirming the formation of N-CQDs [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 3 b, pure ZnO nanorod exhibits an absorption edge at 382 nm, indicating high absorption ability in the UV region. Both samples exhibit strong UV absorption; however, the absorbance edge of the N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite, formed by integrating N-CQD nanoparticles with ZnO, shows a slight red shift compared to pure ZnO, attributed to the synergistic interaction between the two components as shown in Fig. 3 b. This red shift suggests that coating ZnO nanorods with a carbon layer extends ZnO’s optical absorption into the visible and NIR regions [50]. Additionally, the higher absorption intensity in the UV to visible region for N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite than ZnO can be attributed to both the ability of N-CQDs to absorb light and the formation of an electronic interaction between carbon and metal oxide phases. The band gap energies of the synthesized N-CQDs, ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst samples were calculated using the Tauc Eq. (1) as below.

The equation uses Eg, α, hυ, and A to denote band gap energy, absorption coefficient, photon energy (eV), and absorption index [51]. By extrapolating the linear part of the graph of (αhν)2 against photon energy (Eg), the band gap energies were determined as 2.18, 3.29, and 3.27 eV for N-CQDs, ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO samples, respectively (Fig. 3 c and d).

In photocatalytic processes, photoluminescence (PL) study is a complementary and important tool to gain information about charge dynamics such as e-/h+ pair recombination and charge transfer [8]. Therefore, PL spectra of N-CQDs, ZnO nanorods and N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite with excitation at 325 nm were taken and given in Fig. 3 e. While the near-band edge emission for ZnO nanorods appears at 387 nm, a slight red shift to 389 nm is observed for the N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite. Additionally, the emission intensity significantly decreases for the composite compared to pure ZnO, suggesting effective charge transfer at the N-CQDs/ZnO interface, likely due to the formation of an S-scheme heterojunction, which reduces e-/h+ recombination [8,52,53].

To assess the enhancement in photocatalytic performance provided by incorporating N-CQDs into the ZnO structure under UVA irradiation, adsorption (N-CQDs/ZnO), photolysis (UVA), and photocatalysis (ZnO/UVA and N-CQDs/ZnO/UVA) experiments were conducted under previously optimized conditions (10 mg/L TC, 0.5 g/L catalyst, and natural pH of 5.17). The results obtained from the experiments are given in Fig. 4.

Only 11.14 % removal of the pollutant was obtained after 120 min with the TC adsorption on the N-CQDs/ZnO catalyst surface, as shown in Fig. 4 a, without irradiation. It is clear that adsorption does not have an important role in the removal of TC. When UVA irradiation is applied to the TC/water system without the use of a catalyst, the removal efficiency is 33.26 % in 120 min. This demonstrates the importance of the catalyst and UVA irradiation either degrades the TC molecules very slowly and/or does not have enough energy to break the TC bonds [53]. When ZnO nanorods were added to the system together with UVA irradiation, the removal efficiency rose to 54.85 % after 120 min, and to 78.33 % in the presence of N-CQDs/ZnO in the same period. These results align with the optical properties data, indicating that the addition of N-CQDs to ZnO enhances light absorption capacity and promotes the generation of photogenerated charge carriers [15,52,54,55]. Additionally, the nitrogen containing functional groups in the N-CQDs structure make carbon-based materials more polar on the surface, which helps them disperse more easily in an aqueous environment [56]. The photocatalytic performance of semiconductors with N-CQDs is dependent on the carbon content of the composite [8]. By varying the added N-CQDs quantities while maintaining the ZnO ratio constant, the proper carbon content for the N-CQDs/ZnO catalyst was determined as shown in Fig. 4 b. As seen from the figure, the removal efficiencies for 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.08, 0.10, 0.14, 0.28, and 0.57 g of N-CQDs were found to be 61.78 %, 78.33 %, 74.26 %, 70.63 %, 65.93 %, 60.16 %, 50.24 %, 45.21 %, and 35.12 respectively. The results show that 0.03 g of N-CQDs provided the best synergistic interaction with ZnO. One possible explanation for the drop in TC removal effectiveness when the amount of N-CQD exceeds 0.03 g may be a decrease in TC adsorption on the ZnO surface coated with an excess amount of N-CQDs. On the other hand, the amount of light absorbed by the composite has decreased due to competition for light absorption between increasing N-CQD particles and ZnO. Finally, N-CQD particles deposited on the surface serve as a recombination hub for charge carriers, reducing photocatalytic activity [8]. The optimal amount of N-CQDs was found to be 0.03 g throughout the study’s survival phases.

To investigate the effect of temperature on photocatalytic TC degradation, experiments were conducted at optimum conditions and 293, 303 and 318 K. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the removal efficiency, which was 78.83 % at 293 K, decreased to 64.5 % and 46.3 % by increasing the temperature to 303 and 318 K. Increasing the temperature enhances the probability of collision by mobilizing both charge carriers, which may also lead to an increase in e⁻/h⁺ recombination. Secondly, the first stage of heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation is adsorption. If the adsorption is exothermic in character, it causes desorption of both ROS species adsorbed on the surface and TC and its intermediate products without photocatalysis reaction, which results in low TC removal. On the other hand, increasing temperature reduces the amount of dissolved oxygen in the solution, and accordingly, the amount of superoxide radicals formed decreases. The rate of interfacial electron transfer to oxygen may increase. As a result, it can be said that the above-mentioned reasons have additive contributions to the observed change in removal efficiency [57].

A linear pseudo-first-order model was used to study the reaction kinetics of removing TC by various parameters (2) [58].
(2)

A

A

(3)

1 / 2
= ln
2

app
where t is the UVA light irradiation duration, at is the actual TC absorbance value at time t, and A0 is the initial TC absorbance value. Fig. 4 c and d depicts the corresponding kinetic factors (apparent rate constant, kapp; half-life, t1/2; and linear regression coefficients, R2) for various reaction conditions. As can be seen from the figure, R2 values are above 0.9 for all processes, revealing that the pseudo first order model is suitable for all processes and temperatures. The kinetic rate constants (kapp) for TC removal by the systems of N-CQDs/ZnO, UVA, ZnO/UVA, and N-CQDs/ZnO/UVA were 1.10−3 min−1, 3.4.10−3 min−1, 7.2.10−3 min−1, and 1.12.10−2 min−1, respectively, whereas t1/2 values were 693.15 min, 203.87 min, 96.27 min, and 61.89 min in the same order. The N-CQDs/ZnO/UVA system had the highest rate constant (kapp) value and the lowest t1/2, confirming the catalyst’s superior photocatalytic efficiency [58]. On the other hand, kapp values were found as 1.12.10−2, 7.1.10−3, and 4.9.10−3 min−1 for 293, 303 and 318 K, respectively, while t1/2 values were found as 61.89, 97.62, and 141.46 min. This reveals that low temperatures are favored in N-CQDs/ZnO/UVA system.

The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposite was also examined for different pollutants involving four pharmaceuticals (Tetracycline (TC), ciprofloxacin (CIP), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), acetaminophen (ACT)), for two dyes (Basic Yellow 28 (BY28), and Basic Red 46 (BR46)) and for a heavy metal (Ni2+). The studies in this step were performed at their natural pH, with N-CQDs/ZnO dose of 0.5 g/L, pollutant concentration of 10 mg/L. Fig. 4 e compares the removal efficiencies of TC, CIP, SMX, ACT, BY28, BR46, and Ni2+ pollutants (Table S2) over 120 minutes. As shown in Fig. 4 e, the capacities of TC, CIP, SMX, ACT, BY28, BR4, and Ni2+ were 78.33 %, 55.77 %, 64.05 %, 46.97 %, 75.12 %, 93.61 %, and 10.24 %, respectively. The different removal efficiencies obtained for the selected contaminants under UVA irradiation with N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst can be attributed to their different physicochemical properties [59]. The photocatalyst’s remarkable activity in the removal of pharmaceuticals and dyes indicates that N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst can be successfully employed to treat a wide range of contaminants.

The impact of photocatalyst dosage, preliminary system pH, and pollutant content on TC degradation is investigated further. The pH of solution is a crucial component that can affect both oxidative agents and the catalyst surface in photocatalytic oxidation processes [60]. The effects of pH on TC degradation were tested in the range of 2–11, and the results are displayed in Figure S7a. Additionally, the variation of the zeta potentials of the catalyst/water suspension depending on pH was illustrated in Figure S7b. The pH value (pHSYN) at which the surface charge of the particles is zero was determined as 4.32 for the N-CQDs/ZnO catalyst (Figure S7b). The percentage of TC degradation, initially at 9.8 % at pH 2, increased to 40.90 %, 46.28 %, 78.33 %, 61.16 %, 44.08 %, and 40.05 % when the initial solution pH was raised to 3, 4, 5.17 (natural pH), 7, 9, and 11, respectively. The catalyst’s pHzpc value of 4.32 indicates that its surface charge is positive below pH 4.32 and negative above it (Figure S7b).TC has three pKa values (pKa=3.32, 7.78, and 9.7), which influence its hydrolysis process. TC’s molecular structure is TCH3 + at pH 3.3–7.7, TCH2 ± and TCH¯ at pH 7.7–9.7, and TCH2 ¯ at pH > 9.7 [61]. As a result, since both the catalyst and the TC molecules (TCH3 +) are positively charged at pH = 2, it is obvious that they repel each other. Although there is repulsion at pH 3, it is possible to describe an electrostatic interaction that boosts removal effectiveness from 9.80 % to 40.90 % by deprotonating TC molecules and forming negatively charged centers on the surface. At pH 5.17, the removal efficiency rose to 78.33 % due to the establishment of hydrogen bonds between neutrally charged TC (TCH2 ±) molecules and negatively charged catalyst molecules [62]. As pH rose from 5.17 to 7, 9, and 11, removal efficiency decreased. This is due to the fact that both the catalyst surface and the TC molecules are negatively charged, resulting in a repulsive force between them. All experiments were conducted at the natural pH value of 5.17, taking into account the results of the pH influence experiments.

The effect of catalyst dosage was considered in the N-CQDs/ZnO/UVA process. Figure S7c exhibits TC degradation at various catalyst dosages. Higher catalyst concentrations provide more active sites for TC molecules and other forms of reactive oxygen species (ROS) on the catalyst’s surface. As a result, in heterogeneous photocatalytic processes, increasing catalyst dosage typically improves degradation efficiency [60]. As seen in Figure S7c, the TC removal rate increases significantly from 23.14 % to 78.33 % when the catalyst concentration increases from 0.1 to 0.5 g/L, while it was lowered from 78.33 % to 65.25 % when the photocatalyst dosage was increased to 0.6 g/L. The TC degradation by N-CQDs/ZnO may be hampered by an excess catalyst, which limits the solution’s transmittance and photon excitation [63]. Hereupon, the optimal catalyst amount was determined to be 0.5 g/L N-CQDs/ZnO.

The concentration of TC ranging from 5 to 50 mg/L utilizing an N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst pollutants under UVA irradiation were conducted. The degradation percentages for TC concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mg/L were 85.31 %, 78.33 %, 47.70 %, 35.84 %, 28.14 %, and 20.14 %, respectively (Figure S7d). As can be observed, the removal effectiveness falls as the TC concentration rises from 5 to 50 mg/L. When the TC concentration is increased for a fixed amount of catalyst, the previously sufficient catalyst surface area becomes insufficient for pollutant adsorption. While the number of active centers remains constant for a given amount of catalyst, TC molecules or their breakdown products compete with ROS species for adsorption on the surface. Another situation, as the pollutant concentration increases, the solution’s light transmittance drops, resulting in less light absorption, accordingly decline in removal efficiency [15].

Considering the increasing water pollution alongside the rising global demand for clean water, the plant-based and cost-effective precursors used in this study make the N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst a promising candidate for large-scale applications. Additionally, its stability under various conditions, including pH and temperature variations, enhances its efficiency in addressing water pollution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band alignments, Heterojunction type and Photodegradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The presence of the constructed heterojunction significantly boosted the advanced oxidation process, resulting in highly efficient degradation of TC. Fully grasping the energy band structure of the semiconductors in this heterojunction is key to understanding the complex photodegradation process. Valence band-XPS (VB-XPS) analysis played a crucial role in determining band potentials. This analysis provided insights into the heterojunction type formed and the movement of electron-hole pairs during illumination. The charge decay process predominantly relied on redox potential edges within the heterojunction. Initially, VB potentials of all semiconductors were calculated via VB-XPS analysis using the Eq. (4),
(4)
E
NHE
= φ +
E
VB − XPS
− 4.44

ENHE represents the standard electrode potential, and φ is the electron working function of the XPS analyzer (measured as 4.543 for the instrument) [64]. The VB potentials for N-CQDs and ZnO structures were found to be 2.60 V and 2.30 V, respectively (Figure S8a and b). After the consideration of the bandgap of N-CQDs and ZnO from Fig. 3 c and d, CB potentials of all semiconductors were derived as 0.42 eV and −0.99 eV, respectively, using the well-known formula (5)
(5)
E
CB
=
E
VB
−
E
g
Overall, Figure S8c schematically illustrates the energy band alignments of the semiconductors.

The understanding of charge transfer direction at the interface of constructed semiconductors is significantly influenced by their work function (Φ) [65]. Fig. 5 illustrates the work function graphs of N-CQDs, ZnO, and N-CQDs/ZnO obtained through VB-XPS measurements. This relationship can be described by the Eq. (6)
(6)
hv = EK + EB + φ
where hν represents the incident photon energy, EK is the photoelectron kinetic energy, EB is the electron binding energy, and φ denotes the work function of the device, as measured by XPS. The contact potential difference (ΔV) can be determined using the formula (7) [41,66].
(7)
∆ V = Φ − φ

It is derived from the distance between the points of change in binding energies of free electrons (Inflection point-IP1) and the midpoint of the curve corresponding to the Fermi energy distribution (Inflection point-IP2). By using the device’s work function (measured as 4.543 eV), the work functions of N-CQDs, ZnO, and N-CQDs/ZnO nanostructures were calculated as 6.81 eV, 6.45 eV, and 6.64 eV, respectively (Fig. 5 a-c). Comparing N-CQDs to ZnO, N-CQDs have a tendency thermodynamically to gain electrons from ZnO due to its lower Fermi level. Consequently, an internal electric field (IEF) is produced, and is expected to oppose the electron flow thus, directing it from ZnO to N-CQDs. When N-CQDs and ZnO interfaces come into contact, the band edges of N-CQDs bend upwards, and those of ZnO bend downwards to achieve equilibrium between their Fermi levels. (Fig. 5 d). Based on these theoretical calculations, it was assumed that the spatial bending of the band edges between the CB of N-CQDs and VB of ZnO are favorable and facilitating charge flow. Under UV-A irradiation, photogenerated electrons in the CB of N-CQDs and photogenerated holes in the VB of ZnO are promoted by induced IEF. Consequently, the holes in the VB of ZnO and the electrons in the CB of N-CQDs can easily interface to bond materials, resulting in the formation of a typical S-scheme type heterojunction. This S-scheme mechanism facilitates charge separation and transfer, enhancing the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/ZnO (Fig. 5 d).

In photocatalytic processes, ROS are typically the primary contributors to the oxidation of the target pollutant [51]. ROS trapping studies were conducted to assess the contribution of ROS species to the photocatalytic degradation of TC in the presence of N-CQDs/ZnO catalyst and UVA photons ( Fig. 6 a). In the experiments, the [TC]/[scavenger] ratio was kept constant at 1:1. Benzoquinone (BQ) as superoxide radical (
O
2
. −
) scavenger, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as h+ scavenger, isopropanol (IPA) as
OH
free
.
and h+ scavenger, potassium iodide (KI) as
OH
free
.
and
OH
ads
.
scavenger and chloroform (CHCl3) as
OH
ads
.
scavenger were utilized [67–69].

The addition of BQ reduced the removal efficiency from 78.33 % to 27.11 %, indicating that superoxide radicals play the most significant role in TC degradation in this system. Moreover, after adding EDTA to the solution, the removal efficiency decreased to 30.97 %, revealing that holes also play an important role in TC photooxidation. IPA, which reduces the percent TC removal from 78.33 % to 45.57 % by inhibiting the
OH
free
.
radicals and holes found in the solution, reveals that the minor role of free
OH
.
radicals in the degradation.

The removal efficiency, which drops to 46.85 % with the addition of KI, an effective inhibitor of for free and surface
OH
.
radicals demonstrates that the role of
OH
ads
.
radicals is also crucial. Chloroform, a surface
OH
.
radical scavenger, was also added to the solution to discriminate between the roles of adsorbed and free
OH
.
radicals in TC elimination [69]. According to the findings, percent TC removal dropped from 78.33 % to 54.99 %. Accordingly, the efficiency of ROS species in TC oxidation can be ordered as
O
2
. −
> h+ >
OH
ads
.
.

The combined results of the scavenger trials and band alignment calculations provide information on the plausible photodegradation mechanism (Fig. 6 b). Initially, an S-scheme heterojunction is created between N-CQDs with unique electrical and spectral characteristics and ZnO. N-CQDs’ excellent acceptor and carrier properties reduce e-/h+ recombination and increase the production of ROS species [70]. Photons are absorbed by N-CQDs/ZnO, then stimulated by UVA irradiation and photogenerated electrons and holes occur in the CB and VB, respectively. Photogenerated electrons in the CB of N-CQDs and holes in the VB of ZnO are recombined at the heterojunction interface due to ZnO’s higher VB potential than N-CQDs’ CB potential opposing the IEF [71]. The CB potential (-1.05 V) of ZnO is sufficient to generate the
O
2
. −
radical (E
O
2
/ O
2
∙ −
= −0.33 V vs NHE) which is consistent with the efficient charge separation and electron migration obtained in PL analyses [66]. Simultaneously, the holes in N-CQDs’ VB degrades TC molecules, either directly or via the
OH
.
radicals generated by its interaction with H2O. This substantially decreases electron-hole recombination, resulting in higher efficiency in TC removal.

The reusability test of the photocatalyst, along with the preservation of its photocatalytic function, is crucial for its long-term viability in full-scale applications [72]. After the first photodegradation series of TC was completed, the catalyst was separated by centrifugation, washed with water three times, filtered, and dried at 80 ℃ before being employed again in photocatalysis experiments under the same conditions. After five cycles, the TC removal efficiency declined from 78.33 % to 68.27 % (Fig. 6 c). These findings clearly demonstrate the superior long-term stability of the N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst. Over time, the photocatalytic activity of the N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst may have declined due to the incomplete removal of TC degradation byproducts or unreacted TC molecules during the washing process. The XRD diffractogram of the N-CQDs/ZnO photocatalyst, obtained after five cycles, showed no change in peak positions compared to the initial diffractogram as shown in Figure S9.

To understand degradation process and intermediates involved of TC using N-CQDs/ZnO under UVA illumination, a certain amount of sample was taken and analyzed using GC-MS analysis at different intervals (Table S3). The degradation intermediates revealed that mostly acetamide and propionic acid derivatives were observed, and the aromatic ring was decomposed into the alkyl chains in 30 and 60 min. Based on these findings, double bonds fused the aromatic ring of TC may oxidize by the generated ROS species and the further h+, e- and
OH
.
radicals react with those intermediates after the cleavage of CC bonds [67].

The method and catalyst used in this study for TC photodegradation were evaluated for their efficiency by comparing them with previous studies on the same subject, with results shown in Table S4 . The data reveals that the N-CQDs/ZnO nanocomposites exhibited outstanding photocatalytic activity in degrading TC under the specified conditions.

In summary, this research successfully developed a N-CQDs photocatalyst derived from anthraquinone-rich Rheum ribes L. and integrated with ZnO to form a heterojunction structure that exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance for the degradation of TC under UV light. The strong interaction between N-CQDs and ZnO, verified through advanced characterization, resulted in high surface area, improved light absorption and efficient charge separation through an S-scheme heterojunction mechanism. By systematically optimizing key operational parameters, including catalyst concentration, temperature, TC levels, and solution pH, the photocatalytic efficiency was further maximized. Utilizing the highly efficient catalyst, the pollutant scope was expanded to encompass dyes, heavy metals, and various antibiotics, highlighting its broad applicability. Radical trapping experiments indicated that TC degradation likely occurs via a photooxidation mechanism mostly dependent on
O
2
. −
, h+, and
OH
ads
.
while GC-MS analysis revealed the intermediate transition products formed during TC degradation. In addition, five reuse cycles without any loss in degradation efficiency demonstrated the chemical stability and long-term durability of the active sites on the surface of N-CQDs/ZnO. Overall, the findings establish the N-CQDs/ZnO heterojunction as a promising and sustainable photocatalyst for effective antibiotic contaminant removal, offering considerable potential for advanced water purification applications.
